medications.drug_name: Topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone 1%, or betamethasone valerate 0.02% for face/chest)
medications.indication_rationale: A mild/moderate topical corticosteroid calms the local autoimmune inflammation of the superficial flare, as prescribed in the source note. It should be started in-person once the dangerous infective/drug mimics have been excluded — a strong topical steroid applied to misdiagnosed eczema herpeticum or infected skin can worsen it, so it is not appropriate to initiate blindly via telehealth.
medications.dose_route_frequency: hydrocortisone 1% cream (or betamethasone valerate 0.02% cream) applied as a thin layer to affected areas of the face and chest
contraindications_in_this_case: Undiagnosed cutaneous herpes simplex (eczema herpeticum) — topical steroids can worsen HSV infection, so exclusion by examination is required before use

medications.drug_name: White soft paraffin / plain petroleum jelly (emollient barrier)
medications.indication_rationale: Applying a thin layer of plain white petroleum jelly over the crusted areas after gentle washing with cool water creates a protective barrier that eases the burning and prevents the fragile crusts from cracking and bleeding. Safe, patient-applicable interim care.
medications.dose_route_frequency: thin layer to affected areas after gently washing with cool/lukewarm water, as needed

medications.indication_rationale: The patient's anti-dandruff shampoos are mechanically and chemically aggravating an already fragile superficial epidermis and worsening the burning. They must be stopped immediately.
medications.deprescribing_note: Advise ceasing all harsh anti-dandruff shampoos and exfoliating cleansers immediately; substitute gentle lukewarm-water washing and a bland emollient.

medications.indication_rationale: There is no fever, no thick purulent honey-coloured crust and no lymphadenopathy to support significant secondary bacterial infection. Empirically treating this as impetigo before examination is an error of commission that can mask a dangerous mimic; reserve antibiotics for swab-confirmed or clinically evident secondary infection.

Eczema herpeticum is excluded on examination (no punched-out erosions, no systemic toxicity). However, if in-person examination or HSV PCR suggested disseminated HSV, urgent systemic antivirals would become mandatory — hence exclusion must be by examination, not remote reassurance.

medications.drug_name: Systemic corticosteroids / immunosuppressants (e.g. oral prednisolone, rituximab)
medications.indication_rationale: Mild superficial disease does not warrant systemic immunosuppression at this stage, and such therapy must be a specialist dermatology decision after biopsy confirmation — never initiated remotely on a presumptive diagnosis.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop all harsh anti-dandruff shampoos and exfoliating cleansers immediately; wash affected skin only with cool/lukewarm water.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: These products are actively worsening the fragile superficial skin and the burning.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not scrub, rub or pick at the crusts; apply a thin layer of plain white petroleum jelly as a barrier after gentle washing.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Minimising mechanical trauma protects the fragile epidermis, reduces burning and prevents cracking/bleeding.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Acknowledge the patient's fear of a spreading facial infection and explain the plan for urgent in-person assessment and reassurance.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: High aesthetic distress and health anxiety are prominent; addressing them supports engagement and adherence.

patient_education_points.point: This needs to be looked at in person urgently because some serious look-alike conditions (a spreading herpes infection, a bacterial skin infection, or a severe drug/skin reaction) can only be told apart by examining the skin and the mouth and eyes.
Stop the anti-dandruff and harsh shampoos straight away — they are making the burning and peeling worse.
In the meantime, wash gently with cool water only and smooth on plain petroleum jelly to soothe the burning and stop the crusts cracking — do not scrub or pick.

If it is the likely underlying condition, it is an immune condition affecting the very top layer of the skin — not a flesh-eating infection — and it does not cause deep scarring, but the diagnosis is presumptive until a skin biopsy is done.
See your GP within a few days to arrange a dermatology referral and a skin biopsy for long-term control.

follow_up_plan.interval: urgent same-day in-person assessment to exclude the dangerous mimics; then GP review within 3-5 days to coordinate a formal dermatology referral and potential skin biopsy
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: morphology and Nikolsky sign, mucosal status, signs of secondary infection or systemic upset, response to topical care, and arrangement of dermatology referral and biopsy
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: development of mouth/lip sores or blisters, red/sticky/painful eyes, fever, thick yellow-green pus, or redness spreading rapidly across the body

must_include_items: Recognise that a rapidly spreading burning crusted facial eruption cannot be safely diagnosed remotely and arrange URGENT in-person assessment to exclude eczema herpeticum, severe impetigo and Stevens-Johnson syndrome
Advise stopping the anti-dandruff / harsh shampoos immediately
Advise gentle interim skin care (cool-water washing, plain petroleum jelly barrier, no scrubbing or picking)
Safety-net explicitly for mucosal involvement (mouth/eye), fever, and rapid whole-body spread with the correct escalation
Arrange GP follow-up for dermatology referral and biopsy for definitive diagnosis and long-term control
acceptable_alternatives: Referral to an urgent care clinic or dermatology-capable service rather than the GP for the same-day in-person examination
Explaining that a topical corticosteroid is likely part of management once examined, without prescribing it blindly via telehealth
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange in-person examination and instead reassuring the patient remotely
Failing to advise cessation of the aggravating shampoos
Failing to safety-net for the mucosal/febrile/whole-body-spread red flags
errors_of_commission: Diagnosing 'seborrhoeic dermatitis / dandruff' remotely and dismissing the acute flare
Prescribing empirical systemic antibiotics or antivirals without examination
Initiating a potent topical or systemic corticosteroid remotely before eczema herpeticum/infection is excluded
Over-escalating to a 000 ambulance for a non-life-threatening dermatological presentation
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES arranging urgent in-person assessment (not remote reassurance) AND safety-netting for the mucosal/febrile red flags. Dismissing the eruption as dandruff/seborrhoeic dermatitis, or empirically prescribing systemic antimicrobials, is a critical failure regardless of other content.
